titleStmt.title: Serial Fiction: Mapping the Literary Landscape in the C19 United States

author.affiliation: University of Illinois Urbana Champaign, United States of America

keywords.term: network analysis and graphs theory and application
I plan to attend the conference on-site in Lisbon

head.hi: The nineteenth century in America witnessed a transformative period in literature marked by the proliferation of popular periodicals and the serialization of fiction. Using data from the Library of Congress' historical newspaper archive, Chronicling America, I will identify a network of widely-distributed authors of American serialized fiction in the nineteenth century. My research explores serialization’s impact on literary expression and reader engagement.
This project contributes to scholarship at the intersection of fiction, newspapers, seriality, and digital humanities, drawing on frameworks like Ryan Cordell and David Smith’s
Viral Texts: Mapping Networks of Reprinting in 19th-Century Newspapers and Magazines

. Additionally, I build on Christopher Looby’s argument that “the material circumstances of a text’s publication, circulation, and reception impinge consequentially upon attempts at critical understanding” (Looby 2004: 181), and Katherine Bode, who emphasizes that constructing a dataset for digital archive research can serve as an “extended historical argument in and of itself” (Bode 3: 2018). The dataset I have gathered, consisting of half a million digitized pages from U.S. newspapers (1800-1899) sourced from Chronicling America’s database, serves as the first phase of my argument.

To isolate serial fiction from other newspaper content, I developed a python-based text analysis workflow that identifies formal features distinct to these serialized works such as chapter headings and author attribution. By leveraging these formal features I extract relevant text, creating a dataset of serialized newspaper fiction. To analyze authorial prominence, for example, I identify high-frequency bigrams (author names) within close proximity to the term “CHAPTER.” This approach allows me to map a network of newspaper serial fiction, uncovering patterns of printing and reprinting across publishers, dialogue between authors, and insights into contemporary readers' preferences and engagement.
Preliminary findings suggest that significant authors and works, such as Harriet Beecher Stowe’s

, an abolitionist periodical, and later by John P. Jewett and Company in Novel form–are surprisingly underrepresented in American newspapers. This well-known author appears infrequently, and only as “Mrs. Stowe.” Given that much literature was imported from the UK during America’s nascent years, my suspicion that British authors would dominate the list was confirmed, with H. Rider Haggard appearing over 500 times and others like Hugh Conway with several hundred instances. Most importantly, my work is focused on the recovery of then-popular but now-forgotten fiction writers, as well as developing a theory of unfinished fiction and abandoned serial runs.

This poster presentation offers a revised understanding of the 19th-century U.S. literary landscape, providing a more robust and historically-situated "canon" of important serial works, which allow me to theorize the role of seriality in more nuanced and medium-specific ways than previous critics. By employing machine learning tools such as network analysis, I aim to recover the most popular and widely read authors and novels of that era from systematic disregard, and to challenge traditional interpretations centered on canonical texts. Ultimately, this research sets out to redefine the narratives surrounding authorship and readership in 19th-century America. The findings underscore the importance of considering the material and social contexts in which literature circulates, prompting a reevaluation of how we understand literary history and its relationship to broader societal trends.

bibl.hi: A World of Fiction: Digital Collections and the Future of Literary History
Viral Texts: Mapping Networks of Reprinting in 19th-Century Newspapers and Magazines
